description: System integrity audit preconditions — filesystem, process, and log integrity. These are not attack surface conditions. They are system health conditions whose violation indicates compromise, misconfiguration, or degradation. The same tri-state logic applies: REALIZED = condition confirmed good, BLOCKED = condition violated (constraint broken), UNKNOWN = not yet measured.

FI-01.label: system_binary_integrity_confirmed
FI-01.description: Key system binaries (/bin/sh, /usr/bin/sudo, /sbin/sshd, etc.) match their recorded hashes. A mismatch indicates tampering, unauthorized update, or supply chain compromise.
FI-01.evidence_hint: md5sum key binaries and compare to baseline. Evidence rank 2 (build/baseline).

FI-02.label: suid_set_unchanged
FI-02.description: The set of SUID/SGID binaries on the filesystem matches the recorded baseline. New SUID binaries are a classic privilege escalation persistence mechanism.
FI-02.evidence_hint: find / -perm -4000 -o -perm -2000 and compare to baseline. Evidence rank 3.

FI-03.label: no_unexpected_world_writable_dirs
FI-03.description: No world-writable directories exist outside of expected paths (/tmp, /var/tmp, /dev/shm). World-writable directories outside these locations are staging areas for payload drops.
FI-03.evidence_hint: find / -type d -perm -0002 excluding standard temp paths. Evidence rank 3.

FI-04.label: no_executables_in_temp_paths
FI-04.description: No executable files exist in /tmp, /var/tmp, or /dev/shm. Executables in temp directories indicate payload staging, compiled-and-run attacks, or memory-resident implants.

FI-05.label: no_unexpected_recent_system_file_modifications
FI-05.description: No unexpected recent modifications to /etc, /bin, /sbin, /usr/bin. Unexpected changes to system files are indicators of backdoor installation, privilege escalation, or misconfiguration.
FI-05.evidence_hint: find system paths -newer /proc/1 -type f excluding package manager noise. Evidence rank 1.

FI-06.label: file_attributes_nominal
FI-06.description: Critical files (/etc/passwd, /etc/sudoers) do not have unexpected immutable flags. Unexpectedly immutable files may indicate an attacker locking a backdoor in place.

FI-07.label: passwd_shadow_integrity
FI-07.description: /etc/passwd has no new UID 0 accounts and no unexpected shell-bearing accounts since last baseline. New root accounts are the clearest indicator of persistent backdoor access.
FI-07.evidence_hint: Parse /etc/passwd for UID 0 and shell accounts; compare to baseline. Evidence rank 3.

FI-08.label: no_deleted_files_with_open_handles
FI-08.description: No processes hold open file handles to deleted files. This is a classic persistence technique — the file is unlinked but the process memory map keeps it alive.

PI-01.label: process_set_within_manifest
PI-01.description: Running processes match the declared service manifest. New processes not in the manifest require investigation — they may be unauthorized services, implants, or attack tools.
PI-01.evidence_hint: ps aux | awk '{print $11}' | sort -u; compare to baseline. Evidence rank 1.

PI-02.label: no_processes_from_temp_paths
PI-02.description: No processes are executing from /tmp, /var/tmp, or /dev/shm. A process running from a temp directory is a strong indicator of a dropped payload or in-memory implant.

PI-03.label: listening_ports_match_manifest
PI-03.description: All listening TCP/UDP ports correspond to declared services. New listening ports not in the baseline indicate an unauthorized service, reverse shell, or C2 listener.
PI-03.evidence_hint: ss -tnlp | grep LISTEN; compare to baseline. Evidence rank 1.

PI-04.label: root_processes_within_baseline
PI-04.description: Processes running as UID 0 match the recorded baseline. New root processes that are not known system services are indicators of privilege escalation or rootkit installation.
PI-04.evidence_hint: ps aux | awk '$1=="root"'; compare to baseline. Evidence rank 1.

PI-05.label: no_shells_from_service_processes
PI-05.description: No interactive shell processes (bash, sh, zsh) are spawned by service processes (nginx, apache, mysqld, etc.). A shell child of a service process is a near-certain indicator of RCE.
PI-05.evidence_hint: ps -ef; for shell processes trace parent PID to service. Evidence rank 1.

PI-06.label: zombie_count_normal
PI-06.description: Zombie process count is within normal bounds (< 3). Abnormal zombie accumulation indicates a crashing process in a loop, fork bomb remnant, or malware spawning abandoned children.
PI-06.evidence_hint: ps aux | grep -c ' Z '. Evidence rank 1.

PI-07.label: crontab_unchanged
PI-07.description: Crontab content (system and user) has not changed since the last baseline. New or modified cron entries are the most common persistence mechanism on Linux systems.
PI-07.evidence_hint: cat all crontabs | md5sum; compare to baseline. Evidence rank 3.

PI-08.label: no_ld_preload_hijacking
PI-08.description: /etc/ld.so.preload is absent and LD_PRELOAD is not set in the init environment. LD_PRELOAD hijacking is a classic rootkit and credential-harvesting technique that intercepts all library calls.
PI-08.evidence_hint: cat /etc/ld.so.preload; cat /proc/1/environ | grep LD_PRELOAD. Evidence rank 3.

LI-01.label: logging_daemon_running
LI-01.description: A logging daemon (syslog, rsyslog, journald) is running and collecting. Absent logging infrastructure means all log integrity wickets are structurally unknown — this is a fold.

LI-02.label: log_files_growing
LI-02.description: Log files have grown since the last observation. A log file that shrinks outside of rotation windows is a strong indicator of tampering or deliberate log wiping.

LI-03.label: auth_failure_rate_normal
LI-03.description: Authentication failure rate is within declared bounds. Anomalously high failure rates indicate active brute force, credential stuffing, or a compromised system attempting lateral movement.
LI-03.evidence_hint: grep -c 'Failed password' /var/log/auth.log in rolling window. Evidence rank 1.

LI-04.label: no_log_gaps
LI-04.description: Log entries are present in recent time windows. A gap in logging while the system was running indicates log tampering, logging service crash, or deliberate suppression.
LI-04.evidence_hint: journalctl --since '5 minutes ago' | wc -l. Evidence rank 1.

LI-05.label: auditd_active_with_rules
LI-05.description: The audit daemon is running with meaningful rules covering privileged actions. Without auditd, there is no tamper-evident record of file access, privilege escalation, or network connections.
LI-05.evidence_hint: pgrep auditd; auditctl -l | wc -l. Evidence rank 1.

LI-06.label: logs_forwarded_to_remote
LI-06.description: Logs are forwarded to a remote SIEM or log aggregator. Local-only logs can be tampered by an attacker with root access. Remote forwarding provides tamper-evident audit trail.
LI-06.evidence_hint: Check rsyslog.conf for @@ entries; check filebeat/syslog-ng config. Evidence rank 3.

LI-07.label: log_rotation_intact
LI-07.description: Log rotation is configured and running. Absent rotation or abnormally large log files indicate misconfiguration or deliberate suppression of log evidence.

LI-08.label: wtmp_lastlog_intact
LI-08.description: wtmp and lastlog have not shrunk. These files record login history and are a primary target for attackers covering tracks. Shrinkage = likely tampering with utmpdump or wtmpclean.

filesystem_compromise_v1.description: System filesystem shows signs of compromise — binary tampering, unexpected SUID, or payload staging.

process_compromise_v1.description: Running process state shows signs of compromise — unauthorized services, temp-path executables, or shell spawned by service.

persistence_mechanism_v1.description: Persistence mechanism detected — crontab change, new SUID, new UID 0 account, or LD_PRELOAD hijacking.

log_tampering_v1.description: Log infrastructure shows signs of tampering — shrunk log files, gaps, or wtmp modification.

weak_audit_posture_v1.description: Audit and logging posture is insufficient — no auditd rules, no remote forwarding, local-only logging.

full_system_integrity_v1.description: Complete system integrity assessment — filesystem, process, and log integrity all confirmed.
